medications.indication_rationale: The source clinician gave oral hyoscine butylbromide for symptomatic relief of smooth-muscle colicky spasm AFTER a bedside examination had excluded a surgical abdomen. In telehealth it is at most an acceptable symptomatic option once obstruction/peritonism has been excluded in person — it must not be recommended as a substitute for that assessment.
medications.duration: short-term, for the acute episode only
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid while a mechanical bowel obstruction or paralytic ileus is still possible — antimuscarinic antispasmodics can worsen an ileus and mask progression; only appropriate once a surgical abdomen has been excluded by examination.

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids can mask the evolution of a surgical abdomen and worsen ileus/obstipation; recommending them for undifferentiated acute abdominal pain over telehealth is a dangerous error of commission.
If a mechanical obstruction is present, stimulating the bowel risks perforation; laxatives must not be used to 'treat' obstipation of unknown cause.
There is no evidence of infection; antibiotics have no role in a coeliac/dietary flare and would be inappropriate empirical treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict lifelong gluten-free diet once coeliac disease is confirmed — but only start it AFTER coeliac serology (and any confirmatory biopsy) have been taken while still eating gluten, so the tests are not falsely negative.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A gluten-free diet is the only definitive prevention of future flares in coeliac disease; premature commencement can invalidate diagnostic testing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once a surgical abdomen has been excluded in person, gentle heat (a warm compress / hot water bottle) and slow walking for ~10 minutes can ease trapped-gas cramping.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat and gentle movement relax the bowel wall and promote passage of trapped gas — but only after examination has excluded obstruction, not before.

patient_education_points.point: Because I can't examine your tummy over chat, I can't safely rule out a blockage or appendicitis from here — that's why you need a same-day face-to-face check, not just reassurance.

The most likely cause is an inflammatory gut reaction to gluten in someone with coeliac disease, and that usually settles over 12–24 hours — but that has to be confirmed by an in-person examination first.
Don't start a gluten-free diet until your coeliac blood tests (and any biopsy) are done while you're still eating gluten, or the tests can be falsely normal.

Go straight to an Emergency Department if the pain settles into your lower right side, you spike a fever, you start vomiting, or the pain becomes constant and sharp so you can't move or breathe deeply.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment first; then a GP review in 3–5 days to discuss the coeliac serology result and a structured gluten-free dietary plan
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: resolution of the acute distension and pain, the coeliac serology result, confirmation of diagnosis, and commencement of a gluten-free diet with dietitian support
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any red-flag feature — bilious/persistent vomiting, localising right-lower-quadrant pain, fever, or constant sharp pain with abdominal rigidity — means immediate Emergency Department attendance rather than waiting for the GP review

must_include_items: Recognise that a surgical abdomen (obstruction, appendicitis, perforation) cannot be excluded via telehealth and must not be reassured away
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment so the abdomen can be examined
Give explicit safety-netting for the red-flag features that mandate immediate ED attendance
Advise against starting a gluten-free diet before coeliac testing is completed on gluten
acceptable_alternatives: Symptomatic antispasmodic (hyoscine butylbromide) is acceptable ONLY once a surgical cause has been excluded in person
Heat and gentle movement for trapped-gas relief once obstruction is excluded
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for obstruction/appendicitis/perforation features
Not warning that gluten-free diet must wait until after coeliac testing
errors_of_commission: Confidently diagnosing a benign coeliac flare remotely and advising home management without an in-person examination (reproducing the source clinician's bedside-only conclusion)
Recommending opioid analgesia for undifferentiated acute abdominal pain
Recommending an antispasmodic as the primary action before a surgical abdomen is excluded
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment AND safety-netting for the surgical red flags. Remotely diagnosing a benign coeliac flare and advising home self-management is a critical under-triage failure regardless of other content.
